title: Great culture overall but hard to get rid of bad eggs
body: Great culture overall but hard to get rid of bad eggs
pros: Stable. Even in an economic downturn, people still need to eat. HEB does have a generally great culture. Flexible - allowing you to handle life outside of work during work hours when you need to, and no one will bat an eye (because kids get sick, appointments need to be made during work hours, etc. - it's just part of life).
cons: HEB takes a long time to get rid of jerks. They'll move them around to other teams, making them someone else's problem, instead of just putting in the work to document the issues and terminate them. Pay is less than what you would get at a tech company, but the business is stable.

title: Don't expect to get treated well
body: Don't expect to get treated well
pros: pay- they offer quarterly raises
cons: 1. was hired as part time (20hrs max was the agreed upon amount when I was hired). After the first month I started to repeatedly get scheduled for 36-40hrs workweeks. The response was essentially if I didn't find coverage then it still falls on me to work it. 2. management played heavy favorites at this location. It was to the point where it didn't matter if you had the best metrics and worked the hardest. Every shift worked felt like I was walking on eggshells with no broom in sight 3. Metrics- they are so hard on you when it comes to metrics. overall if you're anything less than perfect in their eyes then have fun working harder than everyone else just to get paid the same
role: Former employee - In Store Shopper - Curbside

title: Interesting work with tradeoffs
pros: Sharp engineering teams working in a demanding physical environment with lots of asks for clever solutions. Engaged business partners who cared about the stores, the customers, and the performance of the technology. There were no boring days and I worked on many projects that I'm proud of.
cons: Tolerance for aggressive, old-school managers in IT with tenure, especially for the difficult store-facing engineering space. Over four years, everyday experiences of: - berating and belittling including personal insults to myself and others - demands for personal loyalty - threats of retaliation to myself and others - stated favoritism with engineering leads resulting in leadership team culture issues such as information withholding and fear - territoriality which made it difficult to collaborate with other groups

After one overslept 6am shift, they said I was âprobably going to be fired,â removed all my shifts, then added two claiming it was an accident. They refused to confirm my status or send anything in writing, insisting I come in. When I didnât attend those two shiftsâbecause they told me I was firedâthey later used that to deny me unemployment, saying it was âvoluntary resignation.â and the proof was that I didnât show for those consecutive shifts.

title: Opportunities for advancement, but you better work
body: Opportunities for advancement, but you better work
pros: Individual contributors support each other like a family - it's not a dog-eat-dog competitive environment. If you work hard, have a great relationship with your boss, network/build relationships around the company, and you also show initiative, you will have opportunities for advancement, but don't expect it to be delivered to you--you need to work for it.
cons: So many partners have come into H-E-B (primarily in Digital) from another local tech company that it's feeling like the tech culture is shifting to be what that company was--and that wasn't great.

pros: HEB gods above and beyond for it's employees, but also it's customers. Pay is great considering it's entry level work. I feel valued when I put in harder work than employees that don't care as much. The Coworkers are the best coworkers I've had compared to any other job, we're always there for each other.
cons: Lousy scheduling for the average person. They cater to high schoolers not needing to live off of a paycheck, or retirees that already have savings and can come in whenever. If you want any real hours, then your have to have full availability (6am-11pm), but expect randomized shifts, which is very exhausting. If your schedule is restricted at all, expect to be scheduled less.

title: Barely a step above a sweat shop
body: Barely a step above a sweat shop
pros: Decent pay. Thatâs about it.
cons: No functioning A/C for curbside workers, blatant favoritism, nearly everything meant for employees is broken and this company makes nearly $2B a week off ONE store. Bathroom locks? Broken. Sewage system? Broken. Freezer doors? Broken. Automatic doors to let us inside between orders? Broken. Itâll be 100Â°+ outside and they canât be bothered to supply water. HEB loves to pretend to care about people, but they only care about their customers. Theyâll use and abuse any worker that isnât their best friend. They donât train workers to do what they were hired to do. Me and several other employees were hired to be shoppers and 5 months later we were still waiting to be trained despite talking to everyone from the highest manager to the lowest and only getting the run around.
role: Current employee - In Store Shopper - Curbside

title: Find something better
pros: Easy to learn "Flexible Schedule"
cons: Zero benefits for a part-time working (no health insurance, no sick/vacation hours, no nothing). Flexible schedule means absolutely nothing. Extremely high expectations to get orders out to customers within 7 minutes while consolidating bags on the fly. Dealing with plastic bag shortage. Coworkers leaving work area a mess with carts not neatly pushed back into place, carts blocking walkways, and being in the way chatting up a storm when they should be working on orders. Order Pickers (Shoppers) doing half of the job and not bagging most items despite having the bags to use making the pullers (curbies) (aka me) have to waste time and bag what should've been bagged. Laughably low pay. Work hours can go as low as 4 to 5 hours (aka only one shift).

pros: They work hard to promote those that show a desire to move up within the company. The benefits are great and available to part time partners once they meet a minimum hour requirement each year.
cons: Each store operates different and little room is made for partners to readjust when moving locations. title: Working for HEB
pros: They give consistent raises. A guaranteed 50 cent raise the first half of the year, and a second raise based on performance. Working at HEB there are so many opportunities to practice hospitality, and many positions to develop skills in. Normally if I wanted a new position theyâd train me within the next week if iâm confident I can do so. There are breaks every 2 hours for partners no in management, and with open communication, they care for your wellbeing and offer medical leaves, family medical leaves, and if you just want a break, you can even schedule a week off just make sure itâs before they make the new schedule. Very flexible for students and they value their workers education before their obligation to the job.
cons: Working there can be monotonous, and every so often, some customers demand more than we can do on hand, but that comes with most jobs so other than that thereâs no more cons

title: Good Part-Time Job
pros: I had a good experience with this company. My superiors were all decent people, most of my coworkers were too. It was easy to trade shifts and have a fairly flexible schedule. Customers are of course a mixed bag, but in general it was alright.
cons: If you want to go full-time, we didn't have many opportunities for that. So it's a good job if you don't have rent or you want to make a little extra money, but it's not enough to live on. And if you're trying to go full-time to get more money, it seemed kind of hard to do that. But I was not trying to move up in the company, so I never really asked about it. I'm sure if I'd wanted to, my managers would have helped me out. Also, when scheduling, they did not take into account how many days in a row you worked. If you have an open schedule, you might work Tuesday through Saturday, then immediately get scheduled Monday through Friday. And we usually only got one week (which actually means about 3 days) advanced notice. I was usually able to give away a shift in the middle so I didn't have to work nine days in a row, but a lot of people didn't or weren't able to do that. And sometimes people would get scheduled to close one day at 11:00, then open the next morning at 5:45. Obviously, that's not okay.
